text: This view shows the entry into the left chest exposing the anterior wall of the hilum.

As the hilar pleura opens, the anterior and superior wall of the left superior pulmonary vein is exposed.
The endogia vascular stapler slides through the newly created tunnel, then divides the superior pulmonary vein.

The bronchus was cut circumferentially with scissors, and then the bronchial stump was confirmed by frozen sectioning to be pathologically free of cancer.
Anatomically mobilized bronchus and several subcarinal lymph nodes are dissected before bronchial and vessel reconstruction.
With better viewing of the pulmonary artery, the proximal and distal control of the PA was obtained using non-invasive vascular block clamp.
The partial pulmonary artery that had cancer invading.
Its surface was then resected using scissors.
After confirming the lack of cancer invasion to the margin, the PA was reconstructed with a primary closure using 4-0 Proline.
Through the utility incision, a standard needle holder of 27 centimeters long was easily inserted in addition to two pairs of clamps.
The anastomosis was first initiated at the medial portion of the bronchial cartilage by using a continuous 3-0 absorbable monofilament suture.
Every time the needle of the continuous suture came in from outside the bronchial lumen, the continuous suture was hooked by another silk suture before the next stitch to the outside of the lumen.
The continuous suture was raised by these silk sutures until it was tight.
The second continuous suture anastomosis begins from the posterior portion.
The third continuous anastomosis begins from the anterior portion.
By this method, the continuous sutures could be tightened and the two anastomotic orifices were closed.
